name: Jiufen
description: A hillside mining town northeast of Taipei with narrow stone-stepped alleys, red lanterns, and sweeping views of the Pacific coast. Said to have inspired the animated film Spirited Away. Best visited on a weekday to beat the crowds.
address: Jiufen, Ruifang District, New Taipei City, Taiwan

name: Din Tai Fung
description: The original Xinyi Road location of the world-famous soup dumpling institution. Watching the kitchen team fold xiao long bao through the glass is half the experience. Order the pork, crab and pork, and the shrimp fried rice. Expect a queue.
address: No. 194, Sec. 2, Xinyi Rd, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Addiction Aquatic Development
description: Part fish market, part restaurant complex in Zhongshan. The sashimi and oyster bar draws a serious crowd on weekend afternoons. Grab a tray, pick your seafood, and find a table by the open kitchen. One of the best casual seafood experiences in Asia.
address: No. 18, Alley 2, Lane 410, Minzu E Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Draft Land
description: Cocktail bar by Angus Zou serving all drinks on tap — a rotating lineup of clarified, carbonated, or barrel-aged cocktails pulled straight from the wall. Quick, consistent, and excellent. One of Asia's 50 Best Bars.
address: No. 1, Anhe Rd Sec. 1, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Bar Mood
description: Elegant Taipei cocktail bar with a reputation for precise technique and creative seasonal menus. The space is intimate and unhurried — exactly right for a long afternoon of drinks. One of Asia's 50 Best Bars for several years running.
address: No. 1, Songgao Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: Elephant Mountain
description: Xiangshan — a gentle 20-minute climb up stone steps from the MRT station to a series of rocky outcrops with the most iconic view of Taipei 101 and the city basin. Best at golden hour. Easy enough for sandals; rewarding enough for the effort.
address: Xiangshan, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: Dihua Street
description: Taipei's best-preserved 19th-century commercial street in Dadaocheng, lined with baroque shophouses selling dried goods, fabrics, tea, and Chinese medicine. The street has gentrified tastefully — good coffee shops and design boutiques sit alongside century-old spice merchants.
address: Dihua St, Datong District, Taipei

name: Shilin Night Market
description: Taipei's most famous night market — massive, loud, and relentlessly food-focused. Oyster vermicelli, stinky tofu, giant fried chicken, and bubble tea at every turn. Go hungry, walk slowly, and eat everything that looks good.
address: Jihe Rd, Shilin District, Taipei

name: The Boutique Hotel Taipei
description: A refined small hotel in central Taipei with considered design and attentive service. Well-positioned for the Da'an and Zhongzheng districts, close to the best restaurants and bars in the city.
address: Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Taipei 101
description: The 508-metre tower that defined Taipei's skyline — the observation deck on the 89th floor gives the best elevated view of the basin, the mountains, and the city grid below. The outdoor deck on 91 is worth the extra step. The basement shopping mall has the best food court in Xinyi District.
address: No. 7, Sec. 5, Xinyi Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: National Palace Museum
description: One of the world's great museums — 700,000 objects of Chinese imperial art spanning 8,000 years, from Shang bronzes to Song ceramics to Qing jade. The Jadeite Cabbage and the Meat-shaped Stone are the famous pieces but the painting and calligraphy galleries are the reason serious visitors stay all day. Allow at least four hours.
address: No. 221, Sec. 2, Zhishan Rd, Shilin District, Taipei

name: Chiang Kai-shek Memorial Hall
description: A vast white marble monument in a formal garden at the centre of Taipei — the changing of the guard inside the hall is one of the most precisely choreographed ceremonies in Asia, performed every hour. The surrounding Liberty Square plaza and the National Theater and Concert Hall framing it are equally impressive.
address: 21 Zhongshan South Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Longshan Temple
description: Taipei's most important and atmospheric temple — a working Buddhist and Taoist shrine in Wanhua that has been rebuilt four times since 1738, most recently after WWII bombing. Incense smoke, worshippers consulting the oracle blocks, and a courtyard full of activity at all hours. Free entry and open late.
address: 211 Guangzhou St, Wanhua District, Taipei

name: Ximending
description: Taipei's pedestrian youth culture district in Wanhua — street fashion, Japanese-influenced cafés, independent cinemas, tattoo shops, and the Red House arts complex. The most visually dense neighbourhood in the city and completely unlike the rest of Taipei. Best on a weekend evening.
address: Ximending, Wanhua District, Taipei

name: Yongkang Street
description: The food street that serious eaters gravitate to — Din Tai Fung's original is here, alongside ice cream shops, bubble tea birthplaces, Yongkang Beef Noodle, and the best Japanese restaurants in Da'an District. Walk the length and the surrounding alleys. One of the most rewarding streets in Taipei.
address: Yongkang St, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Huashan 1914 Creative Park
description: A former winery and industrial complex from 1914 converted into Taipei's best creative and cultural venue — independent exhibitions, live music, design markets, good cafés, and outdoor event spaces. Never the same visit twice. Close to the Zhongxiao Xinsheng MRT.
address: 1 Bade Rd Sec. 1, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Songshan Cultural & Creative Park
description: A 1937 tobacco factory complex in Xinyi District repurposed as a design and arts precinct — studios, galleries, a cinema, and the Taiwan Design Museum inside the beautifully restored industrial buildings. The park surrounding the complex is excellent for a lunch break.
address: 133 Guangfu S Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: Daan Forest Park
description: Taipei's Central Park — 26 hectares of woodland, ponds, and open lawn in the middle of Da'an District. The amphitheatre hosts free weekend concerts. The cycling paths, the pond with egrets, and the MRT station that opens directly into the park make it the most liveable green space in the city.
address: Da'an Forest Park, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Tamsui Old Street
description: A riverside waterfront town at the end of the red MRT line — colonial-era Fort San Domingo, the Oxford College campus, a bustling Old Street market, and a waterfront promenade facing the Guanyin Mountain at sunset. The fish ball soup and iron egg stalls on the old street are the classic snacks.
address: Tamsui Old Street, Tamsui District, New Taipei City

name: Yehliu Geopark
description: A headland of extraordinary wind-eroded sandstone formations on the north coast — mushroom rocks, candle rocks, and the famous Queen's Head formation rising from a narrow peninsula. An hour from Taipei by bus. Best visited early morning before tour groups arrive.
address: Yehliu, Wanli District, New Taipei City

name: Wulai
description: An Atayal aboriginal village in the mountains 40 minutes south of Taipei — natural hot spring pools beside a river, a gondola up to the highland tribal village, and a waterfall walk through the gorge. The mochi and wild boar sausages on the main street are the requisite snacks. A perfect half-day escape.
address: Wulai District, New Taipei City

name: Maokong Gondola & Tea Houses
description: A gondola from the Taipei Zoo MRT station up to the Maokong tea-growing hills — glass-floored cabins, panoramic city views, and a hilltop of traditional tea houses where you can spend an afternoon drinking oolong and watching the city spread out below. The night gondola ride is worth the queue.
address: Maokong, Wenshan District, Taipei

name: Raohe Street Night Market
description: Taipei's most compact and atmospheric night market — a single covered street in Songshan that is easier to navigate than Shilin and arguably better for eating. The black pepper bun from the temple entrance stall, the stinky tofu, and the medicinal herb spare rib soup are the benchmarks.
address: Raohe St, Songshan District, Taipei

name: Ningxia Night Market
description: The most local of Taipei's major night markets — a short street in Datong District beloved by Taipei residents for oyster vermicelli, pork rib soup, red bean cake, and taro balls. Far fewer tourists than Shilin or Raohe. Open from 5pm and best around 8-9pm.
address: Ningxia Rd, Datong District, Taipei

name: Fu Hang Dou Jiang
description: The definitive Taipei breakfast — soy milk hot or cold, deep-fried crullers, shao bing sesame flatbreads, and dan bing egg crepes served from a second-floor space above Zhongxiao Fuxing market. The queue is out the door from 7am and sells out by 11am. Worth every minute of the wait.
address: 108 Zhongxiao E Rd Sec. 1, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Yongkang Beef Noodle
description: The restaurant that set the benchmark for Taiwanese beef noodle soup — slow-braised beef shank in a rich, slightly spiced broth with hand-pulled noodles. The queue is permanent and the wait is around 30 minutes at peak times. Order the half-fat, half-lean beef and the cold tofu on the side.
address: 17, Ln 31, Jinshan S Rd Sec. 2, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Ice Monster
description: The original Taiwanese shaved ice institution on Zhongxiao East Road — finely shaved milk ice served with mango, strawberry, or red bean toppings in bowls large enough for two. The mango shaved ice in summer is one of the great Taipei eating experiences.
address: 297 Zhongxiao E Rd Sec. 4, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Simple Kaffa
description: The best coffee shop in Taiwan and one of Asia's finest — World Barista Championship finalist Berg Wu's Da'an café with an obsessive focus on single-origin sourcing and precise preparation. The pour-overs change with the season and the flat white is as good as it gets in Asia. Always a queue on weekends.
address: No. 1, Ln 243, Dunhua S Rd Sec. 1, Da'an District, Taipei

name: W Taipei
description: The design hotel anchor of Xinyi District — a sleek tower with a rooftop WET deck pool bar overlooking Taipei 101 and the mountain skyline. The YEN Chinese restaurant and the WOOBAR lobby bar draw non-guests. Best positioned for the Xinyi shopping and nightlife district.
address: 10 Zhongxiao E Rd Sec. 5, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: Alchemy
description: One of Taipei's long-standing serious cocktail bars in the Da'an District — a focused menu of original and classic cocktails made with precise technique and quality spirits. The bar team has trained and moved through the best bars in Asia. A reliable anchor for a Taipei drinking evening.
address: No. 1, Ln 101, Fuxing S Rd Sec. 1, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Indulge Experimental Bistro
description: A creative cocktail bar and bistro in Da'an District led by mixologist Tim Zheng — the menu reads like a chef's tasting menu, with cocktails built around seasonal Taiwanese ingredients and unexpected flavour combinations. One of the most original drinking experiences in the city.
address: No. 27, Ln 181, Zhongxiao E Rd Sec. 4, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Lin Mao Sen Tea Co.
description: A fourth-generation tea merchant on Yongkang Street, open since 1950, specialising in high mountain oolongs — Alishan, Li Shan, Da Yu Ling — sourced directly from growers they've worked with for decades. No English menu, but the family will guide you through a gongfu tea session if you ask. Buy the vacuum-sealed bags for travel; the quality is exceptional.
address: No. 193, Sec. 2, Xinyi Rd, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Pinglin Tea District
description: A mountain township 40 minutes southeast of Taipei that produces Baozhong — a lightly oxidised oolong with a floral, buttery character found nowhere else. The valley is terraced with tea gardens, the town has a dedicated tea museum, and several small farms open their doors for tastings. Go on a weekday; rent a scooter and explore the ridge roads above the Beishi River.
address: Pinglin District, New Taipei City, Taiwan

name: Alishan National Scenic Area
description: Taiwan's most celebrated high-mountain tea-growing region and one of its most dramatic landscapes — ancient cypress forests, a narrow-gauge forest railway, and sea-of-clouds views at dawn from Zhushan Sunrise Viewing Platform. Alishan oolong, grown above 1,000 metres, is among the finest in Asia. The sunrise train from Alishan station departs before 5am and fills up; book ahead.
address: Alishan Township, Chiayi County, Taiwan

name: Luk Yu Tea House (台北陸羽茶室)
description: Taipei's most traditional gongfu teahouse, open since 1981 in a quietly elegant room in the Zhongzheng District. Named after the Tang Dynasty author of the Classic of Tea, it serves the full canon of Taiwanese and Chinese teas alongside a menu of refined dim sum and Cantonese dishes. The tea service is ceremonial and unhurried — come for a long afternoon.
address: No. 26, Sec. 2, Chang'an W Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Wang Tea Lab
description: A research-driven modern tea studio in Da'an District where the focus is on single-origin Taiwanese teas presented with the rigour of a specialty coffee bar — precise water temperature, brew ratios, and extraction times. The menu changes with harvest seasons; the Dong Ding and Oriental Beauty selections are exceptional. A serious, quiet space for understanding what Taiwanese tea actually is.
address: No. 8, Ln 93, Fuxing S Rd Sec. 1, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Beitou Hot Springs
description: A forested hot spring valley 30 minutes north of central Taipei on the MRT — no taxi needed. The public Millennium Hot Spring charges NT$40 for outdoor pools fed by sodium bicarbonate water. The Beitou Hot Spring Museum (housed in a 1913 Japanese bathhouse) and the Ketagalan Culture Center are nearby. Go on a weekday morning; weekends get crowded.
address: Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Bopiliao Historical Block
description: A preserved stretch of Qing Dynasty and Japanese colonial-era shophouses in the Wanhua District — the oldest part of Taipei — that survived when the surrounding neighborhood was demolished. Dark brick arcades, traditional storefronts, and almost no tourists despite being two minutes from Longshan Temple MRT. One of the most atmospheric and overlooked corners of the city.
address: Kangding Rd, Wanhua District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Dadaocheng Wharf
description: The riverside promenade at the end of Dihua Street where the Tamsui River opens up — a string of container-turned-bars, pop-up food stalls, and live music on weekend evenings. Come at golden hour for the sunset over the river, then walk back through Dihua Street's old tea and fabric merchants as they close up for the evening.
address: Dadaocheng Wharf, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Guanghua Digital Plaza
description: Taipei's multi-floor electronics and component market — a dense stack of stalls selling everything from the latest GPUs and vintage cameras to resistors by the handful. The surrounding streets extend the market with independent shops for audio gear, gaming peripherals, and Japanese surplus electronics. Closest thing to Akihabara outside Japan. Good for camera film, adapters, and things you didn't know you needed.
address: Civic Blvd Sec. 3, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Treasure Hill Artist Village
description: A hillside community of concrete squatter houses that survived a demolition order and was converted into an artist residency village overlooking the Xindian River. Resident artists live and work alongside each other in a labyrinthine cluster of studios, galleries, and rooftop gardens. Almost entirely tourist-free. Go on a weekend afternoon when studios are open — the views of the river and the city are unexpected.
address: No. 2, Ln 14, Zhongzheng Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Zhongshan Boutique Corridor
description: The most design-forward stretch of Taipei — the underground passage between Zhongshan and Shuanglian MRT stations lined with independent boutiques, bookshops, and galleries, opening onto street-level lanes packed with concept stores, vintage shops, and the best independent cafes in the city. The area around Chifeng Street and Nakasi is Taipei at its most interesting. Not a mall; don't treat it like one.
address: Zhongshan N Rd Sec. 1, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Sun Moon Lake
description: Taiwan's largest lake, set in a mountain basin in Nantou County at 748 metres — surrounded by forested peaks, Thao aboriginal villages, and some of the finest high-mountain tea in Asia. Rent a bicycle and circle the 33km lakeside trail; take the gondola to Formosa Aboriginal Culture Village; catch the sunrise from Ci'en Pagoda. Three hours from Taipei by high-speed rail to Taichung and bus.
address: Yuchi Township, Nantou County, Taiwan

name: Taroko Gorge
description: The most dramatic landscape in Taiwan — a marble gorge carved by the Liwu River through the Central Mountain Range, with sheer cliff walls, turquoise water, suspension bridges, and cliffside trails. The Shakadang Trail and Zhuilu Old Road are the best hikes; Swallow Grotto and the Tunnel of Nine Turns are accessible without a permit. Take the train to Hualien; the gorge entrance is 20 minutes by bus.
address: Xiulin Township, Hualien County, Taiwan

name: Houtong Cat Village
description: A former coal mining village in the mountains east of Taipei that reinvented itself around its feral cat population — now numbering over 100. Catwalks (literal elevated walkways for cats), resident artists, and a preserved coal processing facility make this more than a novelty. One hour by train from Taipei; combine with Jiufen for a full day out. Don't bring dogs.
address: Houtong, Ruifang District, New Taipei City, Taiwan

name: Tainan
description: Taiwan's oldest city and its undisputed food capital — 400 years of history compressed into a walkable old city of temples, colonial-era lanes, Dutch forts, and more breakfast and lunch institutions per block than anywhere else on the island. Milkfish congee, coffin bread, shrimp rolls, and dan zai noodles are the essential eating. Two hours from Taipei on the high-speed rail. Plan a full weekend minimum.
address: Tainan City, Taiwan

name: Kenting National Park
description: Taiwan's southernmost tip and only tropical national park — coral reefs, white sand beaches, a year-round surf break at Jialeshui, and the meeting point of the Taiwan Strait and the Pacific. The Kenting Street night market runs nightly. Best in winter when the north is cold and grey; avoid the summer crowds. Four hours from Taipei by high-speed rail to Zuoying and bus, or fly to Hengchun.
address: Hengchun Township, Pingtung County, Taiwan

name: Sanxia Old Street
description: A beautifully restored Baroque-facade merchant street from the Japanese colonial era in New Taipei City — one hour from central Taipei. Sanxia is famous for its indigo dyeing tradition and for angutai (red bean pastries) sold from storefronts that have barely changed in a century. The Zushi Temple next door is the most ornately carved temple in Taiwan. Far fewer tourists than Jiufen with far more authenticity.
address: Sanxia Old Street, Sanxia District, New Taipei City, Taiwan

name: Cijin Island
description: A narrow barrier island off Kaohsiung connected by ferry (NT$15, five minutes) that most visitors skip entirely. Rent a bicycle at the ferry terminal and ride the length of the island — past a historic lighthouse, the shell museum, a sunset beach, and one of the best seafood strips in southern Taiwan. Grilled oysters, sea urchin rice, and wahoo sashimi straight off the boats. The antidote to Kaohsiung's malls.
address: Qijin District, Kaohsiung City, Taiwan

name: Le Palais
description: Taipei's most celebrated Cantonese restaurant, on the 17th floor of the Palais de Chine Hotel in Datong District. Chef Kang Guo-Ming's cooking is deeply classical — Peking duck, bird's nest soup, and dim sum executed with extraordinary precision. The wine cellar and private rooms make this the city's premier destination for serious business dining. Three Michelin Stars.
address: 17F, No. 3, Sec. 1, Chengde Rd, Datong District, Taipei

name: Taïrroir
description: Chef Kai Ho's tasting menu is the most ambitious expression of Taiwanese cuisine in the country — local ingredients (Alishan mushrooms, Yilan duck, wild mountain herbs) treated with French technique and Japanese restraint. The name blends Taiwan and terroir. The 10-course menu changes seasonally and reads like a love letter to the island. Three Michelin Stars.
address: No. 22, Siwei Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: L'Atelier de Joël Robuchon
description: The late Joël Robuchon's counter-dining concept in the Xinyi District — an open kitchen wrapped in a dramatic black-and-red room where guests watch the brigade work. The pommes purée is still the benchmark. One of the finest French kitchens in Asia, staffed and sourced to European standards. Two Michelin Stars.
address: 4F, No. 28, Sec. 1, Songren Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei

name: logy
description: Ryohei Hieda's intimate tasting menu restaurant in Da'an — a Japanese chef cooking with Taiwanese ingredients through a European lens. The menu is refined, narrative, and deeply original: aged Taiwanese fish, fermented Alishan produce, and seasonal desserts that reframe local flavours entirely. Two Michelin Stars.
address: No. 102, Yongkang St, Da'an District, Taipei

name: Molino de Urdániz
description: Chef David Yarnoz brought his Navarra-rooted Spanish cuisine to Zhongshan District with extraordinary results — a tasting menu that channels the tradition of northern Spain while engaging seriously with Taiwanese produce. Technically impeccable, emotionally rooted. Two Michelin Stars and one of the most surprising restaurants in Asia.
address: No. 63, Sec. 2, Dunhua N Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Mudan
description: A counter restaurant in Da'an devoted entirely to tempura — each piece fried individually in refined sesame oil to an impossibly light batter. Seasonal Taiwanese vegetables and seafood treated with the discipline of a Japanese kappo kitchen. One of the best tempura experiences outside Japan. Two Michelin Stars.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: Yu Kapo
description: A Japanese kappo counter in Songshan District — 12 seats facing an open kitchen where the chef sequences a meal of seasonal Japanese and Taiwanese ingredients through grilling, simmering, and raw preparations. Promoted to two stars in 2025. One of the city's most intimate and precisely calibrated fine dining experiences.
address: Songshan District, Taipei

name: Eika
description: A refined Japanese counter restaurant in Datong District — promoted to two Michelin Stars in 2025 — where the kaiseki-influenced menu foregrounds Taiwanese seasonal produce through Japanese technique. The chef's relationship with local farmers and fishermen drives a menu that changes entirely with the seasons.
address: Datong District, Taipei

name: Impromptu by Paul Lee
description: Chef Paul Lee's tasting menu in Zhongshan District is one of Taipei's most theatrical fine dining experiences — a kitchen that blurs modernist technique with Taiwanese nostalgia, built around high-quality local sourcing. The wine pairings are exceptionally well chosen. A perennial favourite among Taipei food lovers.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Mountain and Sea House
description: Chef Yen Wen-Cheng's restaurant in a historic Japanese colonial building in Zhongzheng District — a tasting menu devoted to the revival of pre-war Taiwanese Han banquet cuisine, using heritage ingredients and century-old recipes reconstructed from memory. Also holds a Michelin Green Star for sustainability. Exceptional and deeply local.
address: No. 94-1, Qingtian St, Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Golden Formosa
description: A family-run Taiwanese restaurant in Shilin that has held a Michelin Star since the guide's debut year. Classic Taiwanese home cooking elevated — braised pork rice, steamed fish, and seasonal vegetable dishes executed with the precision of four generations of cooking knowledge. Unpretentious setting, extraordinary food.
address: No. 2, Ln 50, Tianmu W Rd, Shilin District, Taipei

name: The Guest House
description: The Chinese restaurant at the Sheraton Grand Taipei, specialising in Huaiyang cuisine — the refined Jiangnan culinary tradition characterised by delicate knife work, subtle seasoning, and exceptional braised dishes. The Longjing tea-smoked duck and crystal pork terrine are benchmarks. Formal and unhurried.
address: No. 12, Zhongxiao E Rd Sec. 1, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Tien Hsiang Lo
description: The Hangzhou restaurant at the Palais de Chine Hotel — one of the few restaurants in the world specialising in authentic Hangzhou cuisine outside China. West Lake vinegar fish, Dongpo pork, and Longjing prawn are the classics. A quietly magnificent room with cooking that has no peer in Taipei.
address: 13F, No. 3, Sec. 1, Chengde Rd, Datong District, Taipei

name: Paris 1930 de Hideki Takayama
description: Chef Hideki Takayama's classic French restaurant in the Hotel Éclat Taipei — a formal room with a menu rooted in 1930s Parisian grand cuisine. Foie gras en croûte, soufflés, and tableside service done with complete sincerity. One of the most classically French dining experiences in Asia.
address: Hotel Éclat Taipei, No. 370, Dunhua S Rd Sec. 1, Taipei

name: Sushiyoshi
description: An Osaka-style sushi counter in Da'an — the chef trained at the Sushiyoshi flagship in Osaka and brings the same commitment to aged fish, hand-pressed nigiri, and warm-rice technique. One of the most technically exacting sushi experiences in Taipei, in an intimate eight-seat room.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: Kitcho
description: A Japanese kaiseki counter in Da'an — understated and precise, with a menu that sequences through raw, grilled, simmered, and fried courses using Taiwanese and Japanese seasonal ingredients. The dashi is house-made daily; the pacing is deliberate. A serious and deeply satisfying meal.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: Shin Yeh Taiwanese Signature
description: The fine dining expression of the beloved Shin Yeh restaurant group, dedicated to preserving and elevating classic Taiwanese banquet cuisine. Braised abalone, steamed whole fish, and hand-made noodle dishes prepared with premium local ingredients. The most accessible Michelin-starred Taiwanese restaurant in the city — familiar food made exceptional.
address: No. 34-1, Shuangcheng St, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: ZEA
description: The only Latin American restaurant in Taipei with a Michelin Star — a tasting menu that draws on Peruvian, Mexican, and broader South American culinary traditions reinterpreted through Taiwanese produce. Ceviche made with local fish, mole with Taiwanese chiles, and desserts built around tropical fruit. Genuinely one of a kind.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: FRASSI
description: A newly starred Italian restaurant in Zhongshan District that opened to immediate critical attention — hand-made pasta, Italian regional sourcing supplemented by Taiwanese produce, and a wine list focused on natural and artisan producers. One of Taipei's most exciting newer fine dining addresses.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Hosu
description: One of the 2025 newcomers to the Michelin Star list — a Taiwanese tasting menu restaurant in Da'an that earned its star on first eligibility. The menu is rooted in aboriginal Taiwanese ingredients and indigenous cooking traditions, presented with contemporary technique. One of the most talked-about new addresses in the city.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: La Vie by Thomas Bühner
description: German chef Thomas Bühner's restaurant in Zhongshan District — a European tasting menu with a strong Taiwanese ingredient identity, from the former three-starred chef at Osnabrück's La Vie. Technically refined and genuinely ambitious. One of the highest-profile European chefs cooking in Taiwan.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Sushi Akira
description: An Edomae sushi counter in Zhongshan District run by a Japanese-trained chef — aged fish, hand-pressed nigiri, and a progression of small dishes that follows the classical Edomae sequence. A reliable, technically exacting sushi experience in a compact, focused room.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Three Coins
description: A classic Cantonese restaurant in Zhongshan District that has quietly held a Michelin Star across multiple guide years. Roasted meats, dim sum, and whole-animal Cantonese preparations made with extraordinary consistency. Old-school service and a clientele that has been coming for decades.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: T+T
description: A Taiwanese-Japanese collaborative tasting menu in Songshan District — named for the Taiwan-Tokyo culinary dialogue at its core. The kitchen's treatment of Taiwanese ingredients through Japanese technique produces dishes that feel completely original: aged Taiwanese fish, fermented mountain vegetables, and desserts built around local dairy.
address: Songshan District, Taipei

name: Fujin Tree Taiwanese Cuisine
description: The fine dining expression of the Fujin Tree group — a Taiwanese tasting menu in Songshan District that draws on traditional Han and aboriginal cooking alongside classic Taiwanese home cooking. Set in a leafy neighbourhood building, the restaurant has a warmth and ease that the setting reinforces.
address: No. 553, Fujin St, Songshan District, Taipei

name: Ya Ge
description: The Cantonese restaurant at the Mandarin Oriental Taipei — a formal room with a kitchen that takes its Cantonese tradition seriously: whole Peking duck, live seafood, and seasonal dim sum prepared with Hong Kong-level precision. One of the most consistent Michelin-starred hotel restaurants in the city.
address: Mandarin Oriental Taipei, No. 158, Dunhua N Rd, Songshan District, Taipei

name: A Cut Steakhouse
description: The steakhouse at the InterContinental Taipei — a Michelin-starred dry-aged beef programme in a city not known for Western fine dining. US prime and wagyu cuts dry-aged in-house, exceptional Japanese whisky selection, and a wine list that outperforms most standalone restaurants. The most serious beef destination in Taipei.
address: InterContinental Taipei, No. 12, Zhongshan N Rd Sec. 2, Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Ad Astra
description: A contemporary tasting menu restaurant in Zhongshan District — minimal interior, focused menu, and a kitchen that updates its dishes constantly around what's seasonal and local. One of the quieter Michelin-starred addresses in Taipei, better known among regulars than visitors.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: NOBUO
description: A tasting menu restaurant in Zhongzheng District earning its star through disciplined, personal cooking that draws on the chef's Japanese training and deep engagement with Taiwanese produce. Small, quiet, and without the scene of Zhongshan's clusters — the food is the focus entirely.
address: Zhongzheng District, Taipei

name: Chuan Ya
description: A newly starred Sichuan restaurant in Xinyi District — one of the very few Sichuan restaurants in the world to hold a Michelin Star. The kitchen executes the full breadth of Sichuan technique: mapo tofu, twice-cooked pork, and fish-fragrant aubergine made with properly sourced doubanjiang and Sichuan peppercorns.
address: Xinyi District, Taipei

name: Sushi Kajin
description: A 2025 newcomer to the Michelin Star list — an Edomae sushi counter in Zhongshan District that earned immediate recognition for its fish aging programme and the quality of its Taiwanese-sourced seafood alongside imported Japanese fish. One of the best new sushi openings in recent years.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Motoichi
description: A 2025 newly starred Japanese restaurant in Da'an District — a refined counter experience built around seasonal Taiwanese and Japanese ingredients treated with the full range of Japanese cooking technique. One of several exciting new addresses in the 2025 guide that signals the continued elevation of Taipei's Japanese dining scene.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: Ken Anhe
description: An intimate Japanese kaiseki counter in Da'an — a 10-seat room where the menu unfolds over two hours through raw, grilled, steamed, and simmered courses using the finest available local and imported ingredients. Known for exceptional dashi, pristine sourcing, and a sake programme that is among the best in the city.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: De Nuit
description: A French tasting menu restaurant in Da'an District — a quiet, candle-lit room where the cooking is classic in technique and contemporary in sensibility. The chef's engagement with Taiwanese produce gives the menu a local identity without forcing it. One of the more romantic and understated fine dining addresses in Taipei.
address: Da'an District, Taipei

name: Mipon
description: A Taiwanese restaurant in Zhongshan District that has held a Michelin Star since early in the guide's history — a menu devoted to the refined expression of Taiwanese cuisine using premium local ingredients: Yilan duck, Alishan bamboo shoots, and wild river fish. Less formal than Mountain and Sea House; equally committed.
address: Zhongshan District, Taipei

name: Pier-2 Art Center
description: A former warehouse complex by the harbour in Kaohsiung's Yancheng District, converted into the most significant arts and creative hub in southern Taiwan. The district spans three warehouse zones along the waterfront: colourful murals, independent studios, design shops, and a casual food and drink scene. The outdoor spaces and rail tracks running through the complex give it a genuinely industrial character that most creative parks in Taiwan lack. Best on a weekend when the studios are open.
address: No. 1, Dayong Rd, Yancheng District, Kaohsiung City, Taiwan

name: Chimei Museum
description: The most extraordinary private museum in Taiwan and one of the more spectacular in Asia — a Baroque palace in Tainan's Rende District built by industrialist Shih Wen-long to house his personal collection of Western art, musical instruments, natural history specimens, and historical weapons. The violin collection alone is world-class. The building, designed to look like a Versailles-inspired palace with reflecting pools and gardens, is a spectacle before you go inside. A full day is barely enough.
address: No. 66, Wenhua Rd, Rende District, Tainan City, Taiwan

name: Miniatures Museum of Taiwan
description: A basement museum in Zhongshan District devoted entirely to miniature dioramas and scale models of extraordinary precision — dollhouses, period room settings, European street scenes, and fantasy environments, all built at scales from 1:6 to 1:144. The craftsmanship on display is genuinely staggering. A niche that rewards anyone who pays attention; the detail work visible through a magnifying glass is a different experience from what you see first.
address: B1, No. 96, Jianguo N Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: MOCA Taipei
description: The Museum of Contemporary Art Taipei — housed in a beautifully preserved 1921 Japanese colonial-era elementary school building in Datong District. The permanent collection is light; the programming is the point, with consistently ambitious temporary exhibitions of Taiwanese and international contemporary art. One of the better-curated contemporary institutions in the city. The building itself, with its red brick and colonnaded arcade, is worth visiting.
address: No. 39, Changan W Rd, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei Fine Arts Museum
description: The first modern art museum in Taiwan — opened in 1983 in a grid-form white building near the Grand Hotel in Zhongshan District. The permanent collection covers Taiwanese modern and contemporary art from the Japanese colonial period to the present; the temporary exhibition programme is the strongest in the country and regularly brings in major international shows. The sculpture garden outside is free to walk through at any time.
address: No. 181, Zhongshan N Rd Sec 3, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Yongle Market
description: A five-storey fabric and textile market in the Dadaocheng neighbourhood — the definitive destination for fabric sourcing in Taipei, with hundreds of vendors selling silk, linen, cotton, embroidery, and trimmings. The ground floor is lively wet market; upper floors are dedicated to cloth and accessories. The surrounding Dihua Street area is one of the most atmospheric streets in Taipei: Baroque shophouses, dried goods merchants, and Chinese medicine shops.
address: No. 21, Dihua St Sec 1, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Wufenpu Market
description: The wholesale and retail fashion market in Songshan District that has been the engine of Taiwan's garment trade since the 1950s — hundreds of stalls across a dense urban block selling clothing, accessories, and fast fashion at prices well below department store retail. The professional wholesale hours are early morning; the afternoon is more accessible for casual shoppers. Songshan Station on the MRT drops you directly at the edge of the market.
address: Wufenpu, Songshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Blueprint Cultural Park
description: A creative park in Tainan's West Central District built within the former Taiwan Southern District Court complex — the name references the blue blueprint mural that originally put the site on the cultural map. Independent design shops, print studios, cafés, and event spaces spread through restored Japanese colonial-era buildings and courtyards. Quieter and less commercial than Huashan in Taipei, with a stronger local creative identity. The evening atmosphere is particularly good.
address: No. 247, Ximen Rd Sec 1, West Central District, Tainan City, Taiwan

name: Hayashi Department Store
description: A 1932 Japanese-era department store in central Tainan, beautifully restored and reopened in 2014 as the only surviving Japanese colonial department store in Taiwan. The building is the main event: a five-storey Art Deco structure with a rooftop shrine to the god of business and an elevator that still operates. The retail inside focuses on Tainan local brands, food, and crafts — better quality than most souvenir shops. One of the essential stops on any Tainan visit.
address: No. 63, Zhongzheng Rd, West Central District, Tainan City, Taiwan

name: Red House
description: An octagonal red-brick market building from 1908 in the heart of Ximending — the oldest market structure in Taipei, converted into an arts and performance venue. The main building houses independent designers and exhibition space; the surrounding arcade has become the social hub of Taipei's LGBTQ+ community, with outdoor bars and a relaxed street scene that spills across the plaza on weekend evenings. Free to wander through during the day; livelier after dark.
address: No. 10, Chengdu Rd, Wanhua District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Dream Mall
description: The largest shopping mall in Taiwan — a 300,000 square metre complex in Zuoying District, Kaohsiung, with its own Ferris wheel on the rooftop that has become a Kaohsiung landmark. Anchored by major department stores and a cinema; the food court runs across multiple levels with a range that makes it a genuine destination for eating rather than just retail. The harbour and MRT Zuoying Station access make it easy to combine with other southern Kaohsiung sights.
address: No. 789, Zhonghua 5th Rd, Zuoying District, Kaohsiung City, Taiwan

name: Miramar Entertainment Park
description: A shopping and entertainment complex in Zhongshan District anchored by the 80-metre observation Ferris wheel that has become one of Taipei's most recognisable landmarks — especially at night, when it is fully illuminated. The mall itself is upscale retail; the Ferris wheel is the draw, with gondolas offering clear views across the city on good-weather days. The Jiannan Road MRT station is immediately adjacent.
address: No. 77, Jingye 3rd Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Syntrend Creative Park
description: A multi-storey technology and entertainment complex adjacent to Guanghua Digital Plaza in Zhongzheng District — less about hardware than Guanghua and more about gaming, anime, figures, and the broader geek culture ecosystem. Pokemon Center Taiwan is here; so are concept stores for major game publishers, figure shops, and a gaming lounge. The building also has event spaces that host gaming tournaments and brand activations. Effectively Guanghua's more contemporary counterpart.
address: No. 16, Civic Blvd Sec 3, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Breeze Nan Shan
description: The newest and most design-forward of the Breeze Group's Taipei mall properties — a glass tower in Xinyi District adjacent to the Taipei World Trade Center. The retail skews luxury and upscale; the food hall in the basement is one of the better in the city. The roof terrace has direct views of Taipei 101 from a less-crowded angle than most 101-adjacent spots. Easy walking distance from Xinyi Anhe MRT.
address: No. 100, Songren Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Xpark Aquarium
description: Taiwan's most sophisticated aquarium — opened in 2020 in the Zhongli District of Taoyuan, about 40 minutes from central Taipei. The exhibits are genuinely impressive: a large penguin colony, a jellyfish tunnel, shark tank, and a design-forward approach to display that puts it ahead of most aquariums in the region. The partnership with Yokohama Hakkeijima Sea Paradise shows in the curation. Worth the trip out of Taipei for families or ocean enthusiasts.
address: No. 1, Qingpu Rd, Zhongli District, Taoyuan City, Taiwan

name: Taipei Children's Amusement Park
description: A classic Taipei institution near Jiantan MRT in Zhongshan District — an old-school amusement park with a roller coaster, carousel, bumper cars, and a small train that loops the grounds. The rides are modest by international standards but the park has the kind of nostalgic, unhurried atmosphere that larger parks have lost. Inexpensive by any measure; adjacent to Miramar and a reasonable base for a northern Taipei day.
address: No. 55, Jingye 3rd Rd, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Leofoo Village Theme Park
description: Taiwan's most popular theme park — in Guanxi Township in Hsinchu County, about 70 minutes from Taipei by bus or car. The park is split into four themed zones: African Safari, Wild West, Arabian, and South Pacific, with roller coasters and thrill rides alongside one of the better zoo sections in Taiwan. The safari zone allows close encounters with giraffes and rhinos from an open truck. A full day is required; most visitors come for the combination of rides and animals.
address: No. 700, Wenhua Rd, Guanxi Township, Hsinchu County, Taiwan

name: Banqiao
description: The modern administrative centre of New Taipei City — a city in its own right that has grown up around a major rail and MRT interchange, with a grid of wide boulevards, large shopping malls, and government buildings. The Lin Family Mansion and Garden, one of the finest traditional Taiwanese garden complexes, is here and often missed by visitors who don't venture beyond Taipei proper. The food scene around the old Banqiao night market area remains strong and less touristed than Taipei equivalents.
address: Banqiao District, New Taipei City, Taiwan

name: Gongguan
description: The student neighbourhood anchored by National Taiwan University in Zhongzheng District — a dense commercial strip around Gongguan MRT station with bookshops, independent cafés, cheap eats, and one of Taipei's better night markets. The Shida Night Market and NTU campus are both walkable. The energy is young and unpretentious; prices are the lowest in the inner city. Good base for exploring the Gongguan and Guting neighbourhoods, which have a different character from the Daan and Xinyi crowds.
address: Gongguan, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Tianmu
description: A residential neighbourhood in northern Shilin District with a notably relaxed and international character — the long-established expat community has shaped a strip of foreign restaurants, international grocery stores, and low-key bars along Zhongshan North Road. The Japanese community presence is particularly strong, visible in the food, shops, and general atmosphere. Cooler temperatures than central Taipei in summer and easy access to Yangmingshan make it a good base for a different pace of Taipei living.
address: Tianmu, Shilin District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: The Wall Live House
description: The most important indie rock venue in Taiwan — a basement venue at the Gongguan end of Roosevelt Road that has launched or hosted every significant act in the Taiwanese indie scene since the early 2000s. The room is small and loud in the best way; the booking reflects a genuine curatorial taste for alternative, post-rock, metal, and experimental music. The surrounding Gongguan neighbourhood provides the right pre-show energy.
address: B1, No. 200, Roosevelt Rd Sec 4, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Legacy Taipei
description: The largest and most prominent live music venue in Taipei — located inside Huashan 1914 Creative Park with a capacity that bridges the gap between intimate club shows and arena acts. The booking covers the full range of popular music with a strong track record for bringing in international touring artists alongside the best domestic acts. The creative park setting means there are restaurants and bars nearby for before and after.
address: Hall 5A, Huashan 1914 Creative Park, No. 1, Bade Rd Sec 1, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: PIPE Live Music
description: A live music venue built beneath the Fuhe Bridge on the Xindian River in Gongguan — the industrial setting under the elevated structure gives it an atmosphere no purpose-built venue could manufacture. Programming focuses on indie rock, punk, and metal, with a loyal following from the student population of the surrounding area. The riverside location is genuinely atmospheric on warm evenings; the DIY aesthetic is part of what makes it feel necessary.
address: Under Fuhe Bridge, Gongguan, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Riverside Live House
description: A basement live music space in the Gongguan neighbourhood with a long history as one of Taipei's foundational indie venues — smaller and more intimate than Legacy, rawer than The Wall. The programming spans indie rock, folk, and singer-songwriter acts with a focus on supporting local and regional Taiwanese music. The bar is unpretentious; the crowds are genuinely interested in the music rather than the scene.
address: B1, Roosevelt Rd Sec 4, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Revolver
description: A rock bar in Da'an District spread across multiple floors, including a rooftop terrace that has become one of the more reliably good outdoor bar spaces in central Taipei. The music policy leans rock, indie, and alternative; the crowd is international and without the pretension of the Xinyi clubs. The rooftop is the move in good weather — open-air, cold beer, and a view over the Da'an roofscape.
address: Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Kafka by the Sea
description: A Murakami-inflected cocktail bar in Da'an District — moody lighting, a carefully selected soundtrack, and a drinks programme that takes the craft seriously without the attitude. The name sets the tone: literary, slightly melancholy, comfortable with staying a long time. One of the better bars in Taipei for an evening that doesn't need to be anything in particular.
address: Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Sappho Live Jazz
description: A jazz bar on Zhongshan North Road with a long-running live programme — one of the better rooms in Taipei for hearing jazz performed live in a setting that takes the music seriously. The performances run through the week; weekends draw stronger lineups. The bar itself is comfortable and well-stocked. The Zhongshan District location puts it close to the gallery strip and a good cluster of neighbourhood restaurants.
address: No. 63, Zhongshan N Rd Sec 2, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Blue Note Taipei
description: The Taiwanese outpost of the legendary New York jazz institution — a proper jazz club in the Xinyi District with the production values and booking ambition the Blue Note name demands. International and domestic jazz acts perform multiple sets nightly. The room is handsomely designed; the food and drink programme is more serious than most music venues. Reservations are advisable for the better shows.
address: Xinyi District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Brown Sugar Jazz Club
description: A jazz and R&B club in Da'an District with a warm, loungey atmosphere and a live programme that draws a more dressed-up crowd than the indie venues. The house band is consistently good; the guest roster covers soul, funk, and jazz. The kitchen turns out food worth eating rather than the afterthought of most music venues. A more adult and relaxed jazz experience than Blue Note's formal concert setting.
address: 4F, No. 218, Zhongxiao E Rd Sec 4, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Pawnshop
description: A serious cocktail bar in Da'an District — the drinks programme is ambitious and technique-forward without the showmanship that makes some craft bars exhausting. The space is compact and well-designed; the bartenders know what they're doing. One of the addresses that has elevated Taipei's cocktail bar scene beyond the hotel bar standard. Best approached with a willingness to let the bartender suggest something.
address: Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: FINAL Taipei
description: One of Taipei's leading electronic music clubs — a venue that takes the music and the production seriously, with a sound system and lighting rig that matches international club standards. The programming covers house, techno, and electronic music with a mix of local and international DJs. The crowd is younger and more fashion-conscious than the rock venues but the focus on the music is genuine.
address: Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Korner
description: A foundational Taipei club for underground electronic music — one of the venues most responsible for building the city's dance music culture over the past decade. The programming leans toward techno, bass music, and more experimental electronic forms; the resident DJs and guest bookers have a consistent taste level. The space is deliberately unpolished; the sound system is not.
address: Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: LIVE WAREHOUSE
description: The primary live music venue in Kaohsiung — a warehouse-format space that has established itself as the southern Taiwan anchor for touring acts and the strongest local Kaohsiung music scene. The industrial setting is an honest fit for the rock and indie programming. The venue fills the gap between intimate bar shows and the arena, giving Kaohsiung audiences access to acts that would otherwise not venture south of Taichung.
address: Kaohsiung City, Taiwan

name: Legacy Taichung
description: The Taichung outpost of the Legacy brand — a mid-sized live music venue in central Taiwan that serves as the main stop between Taipei and Kaohsiung for domestic and international touring acts. The production quality matches the Taipei venue; the crowd is the dedicated central Taiwan music audience that tends to be more committed precisely because they have fewer options. Worth checking the calendar if you're spending time in Taichung.
address: Taichung City, Taiwan

name: Lungshan Temple
description: The most important temple in Taipei and one of the most visited in Taiwan — a Qing Dynasty foundation rebuilt after WWII bombing in the heart of Wanhua, dedicated primarily to Guanyin with dozens of other Buddhist and Taoist deities sharing the complex. The dragon pillars, the bronze work, and the carved roof are extraordinary; the continuous incense smoke and the density of worshippers at all hours make it a genuinely alive rather than merely historic space. Free to enter; remove hats and be respectful.
address: No. 211, Guangzhou St, Wanhua District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Yangmingshan National Park
description: The volcanic national park directly north of Taipei — an extraordinary resource for a capital city: active fumaroles, caldera lakes, hot springs, and 50+ hiking trails within an hour of the city centre. Cherry blossom and azalea season (February to April) draws the biggest crowds; summer is pleasantly cooler than the city below. The No. 260 bus from Jiantan MRT runs directly to the park. Xiaoyoukeng, Qingtiangang, and Seven Stars Peak are the three essential destinations inside.
address: Yangmingshan National Park, Shilin District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Sun Yat-sen Memorial Hall
description: A massive ceremonial complex in Xinyi District dedicated to the founder of the Republic of China — the main hall contains a bronze statue guarded by an honour guard that changes on the hour in a ceremony worth watching. The surrounding parkland is one of the most used public open spaces in central Taipei: joggers, tai chi practitioners, and skateboarders coexist around the reflecting pools. Free admission; the rooftop terrace has a direct view toward Taipei 101.
address: No. 505, Renai Rd Sec 4, Xinyi District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Beitou Hot Spring Area
description: The most accessible hot spring district from Taipei — a valley in northern Beitou with public and private facilities ranging from free outdoor foot-soaking pools to century-old Japanese ryokan and modern luxury spas. The hot spring water here is sodium bicarbonate (white sulfur), which is gentler than the radium-bearing spring at Hell Valley. The Hot Spring Museum, the Public Library, and Thermal Valley are all within fifteen minutes' walk of each other. Best on a weekday to avoid weekend crowds.
address: Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Xingtian Temple
description: The most actively worshipped temple in Taipei — dedicated to Guan Di, the god of business and righteousness, and perpetually crowded with Taipei's business community seeking blessings and advice. The volunteer exorcists (tangki) who remove bad luck by brushing worshippers with blessed items are a distinctive feature. The atmosphere is practical and bustling rather than ceremonially solemn. Located on Minquan East Road; Xingtian MRT station is directly outside.
address: No. 109, Minquan E Rd Sec 2, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Baoan Temple
description: The finest example of traditional Taiwanese temple architecture in Taipei — a Qing Dynasty complex dedicated to Baosheng Dadi, the god of medicine, that received a UNESCO Asia Pacific Award for Excellence in Conservation after a community-led restoration. The craftsmanship of the stone carving, wood carving, and painted murals is extraordinary, with competing teams of craftsmen embedding their signatures into the work. Adjacent to the Confucius Temple; both are worth combining in a single visit.
address: No. 61, Hami St, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Confucius Temple
description: The main Confucius Temple in Taipei — a Ming Dynasty-style complex built in 1939 with the characteristic restraint and absence of deity statues that distinguishes Confucian architecture from the exuberant Taiwanese Buddhist and Taoist temples. The annual Teacher's Day ceremony on September 28 features traditional music and dance rituals that have been performed here for centuries. The walled compound and the tranquil gardens provide a quieter counterpoint to the more intense Baoan Temple directly next door.
address: No. 275, Dalong St, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Xiahai City God Temple
description: The most famous City God temple in Taiwan — a small, intensely active shrine at the heart of the Dadaocheng historic district with a matchmaking deity (Yue Lao) who draws pilgrims from across the island. The ritual involves incense, red thread, and written petitions; the temple staff will guide you through it. The surrounding Dihua Street area is the best reason to come: dried goods merchants, the Yongle fabric market, and the best preserved Baroque shophouse streetscape in Taipei.
address: No. 61, Dihua St Sec 1, Datong District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Linjiang Night Market
description: Also known as Tonghua Night Market — the most neighbourhood-feeling of the major Taipei night markets, running along two connected lanes in Da'an District. Less touristed than Shilin or Raohe; the crowd is almost entirely local residents shopping for dinner or snacking on the street. The stinky tofu, oyster omelette, and scallion pancakes here are reliably good. A short walk from Linan Street MRT; best on weekday evenings when it hasn't been discovered for the night.
address: Tonghua St, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Shida Night Market
description: A student-oriented night market around Shida Road near National Taiwan Normal University — smaller and more low-key than the major night markets after stricter city licensing in the 2010s reduced its footprint. The food quality is good and the prices are student-calibrated. The surrounding area has a density of language schools and a significant expat resident population, which gives the neighbourhood a multilingual energy distinct from more residential night market districts.
address: Shida Rd, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Gongguan Night Market
description: The night market cluster around Gongguan MRT station, serving the massive National Taiwan University student population — cheap eats, bubble tea shops, bookstores, and the kind of dense commercial energy that comes from feeding tens of thousands of students. The Roosevelt Road stretch toward Xindian has good Japanese-style ramen and beef noodle shops that stay open late. One of the least tourist-oriented eating areas in central Taipei.
address: Gongguan, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei Zoo
description: The largest zoo in Asia by land area — spread across 165 hectares in the Wenshan hills with over 400 species. The giant panda twins from mainland China are the perennial headline attraction with timed-entry viewing. The African savanna section, the koala enclosure from Australia, and the Taiwan native species area are all strong. The Maokong Gondola terminal is directly outside the main gate; combining a zoo visit with gondola tea houses makes for a full day.
address: No. 2, Xinguang Rd, Wenshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: National Taiwan Museum
description: Taiwan's oldest public museum — established in 1908 during the Japanese colonial era and housed in a Neoclassical building on the north side of 228 Peace Memorial Park. The natural history and anthropology collections are extensive; the building itself, with its copper dome and colonnaded portico reflected in the park pond, is one of the finest colonial-era public buildings in Taipei. The adjacent South Branch in a renovated 1930s railway warehouse is worth combining.
address: No. 2, Xiangyang Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: National Museum of History
description: A Chinese art and artifacts museum in a traditional palace-style building near the CKS Memorial Hall — covering Chinese history from the Shang Dynasty through the 20th century with a focus on bronze vessels, ceramics, and painting. The garden outside the building has a good view across the Botanical Garden pond. Note: the museum has undergone renovation periods; check current opening status before visiting.
address: No. 49, Nanhai Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Beitou Hot Spring Museum
description: Housed in the most beautiful building in Beitou — a 1913 Japanese colonial public bathhouse designed in the style of a Taiwanese vernacular building fused with Japanese resort architecture, with tatami rooms, coloured glass, and a central thermal pool now preserved under glass. The museum documents the history of Beitou's hot spring culture from the Japanese era through today. Free admission; easily combined with the Public Library and Thermal Valley on the same walk.
address: No. 2, Zhongshan Rd, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Thermal Valley
description: A jade-green hot spring pool that reaches 100°C at its source — the colour comes from the high concentration of radium salts that make Beitou's spring chemically unique in the world. The eerie steam rising from the opaque turquoise surface and the surrounding forested slopes create a genuinely uncanny atmosphere. Free to view from the path around the pool; no swimming or soaking. A short walk uphill from the Hot Spring Museum.
address: No. 30, Zhongshan Rd, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Beitou Public Library
description: The first green building library in Asia — a 2006 structure on stilts in a forested valley near the Beitou hot springs, built almost entirely from recycled and sustainable materials with a living roof and passive solar design. Worth visiting even if you don't need a book: the building's relationship with the surrounding trees and the light through the wooden screens makes it one of the most pleasant public spaces in the city. Free to enter; bring something to read.
address: No. 251, Guangming Rd, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Yangming Park
description: The most accessible and manicured section of Yangmingshan National Park — formal flower gardens, fountains, and well-maintained paths that draw the biggest crowds during cherry blossom season (late January to March) and calla lily season (March to April). The park is directly served by bus from Jiantan MRT. Less wild than Qingtiangang or Xiaoyoukeng but the easiest entry point into the Yangmingshan experience for first-time visitors.
address: Yangming Park, Shilin District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Qingtiangang Grassland
description: A high-altitude grassland plateau in Yangmingshan where feral water buffalo have grazed for generations — an unexpected pastoral landscape within an hour of central Taipei. The 360-degree views across the Taipei Basin and toward the Pacific on clear days are among the best in the region. The surrounding trails connect to Datunshan and the Seven Stars Peak circuit. Mist rolls in quickly; bring a layer regardless of the weather in the city below.
address: Qingtiangang, Yangmingshan National Park, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Xiaoyoukeng
description: The most dramatic volcanic feature in Yangmingshan — an active fumarole field where sulfuric steam vents from cracks in the pale grey ground, mineral deposits crust the rocks in yellow and white, and the smell of sulfur hangs in the air. The terrain around the main vent area looks genuinely lunar. Trails lead up to Seven Stars Peak (the highest point in the Taipei area) from here. The visitor centre explains the volcanic geology well.
address: Xiaoyoukeng, Yangmingshan National Park, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Zhuzihu
description: A scenic valley basin in Yangmingshan most famous for calla lily season (March to April) when the terraced farm fields turn white with blooms — the visual effect, with the volcanic peaks behind, is one of Taiwan's most photographed spring scenes. The same farms also grow taro; the restaurant food in the area is mountain-fresh and worth stopping for. The village itself has a genuine working-farm character that survives despite the seasonal tourist crowds.
address: Zhuzihu, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Maokong Tea Area
description: The tea-growing hillside district above the Taipei Zoo in Wenshan District — the source of Taipei's most famous teas, including Tieguanyin and Muzha Tieguanyin. Dozens of tea houses cling to the hillside with views over the city; the experience of sitting with a pot of freshly brewed local tea while looking at the Taipei skyline at dusk is one of the best things you can do in the city. The gondola from the zoo makes access straightforward.
address: Maokong, Wenshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Yinhe Cave
description: A waterfall and cave temple complex in the forested hills of Wenshan District — a small shrine built into a cave behind a waterfall, surrounded by lush subtropical vegetation and accessible via a short hiking trail. The combination of the natural setting and the incense smoke drifting from the cave is genuinely atmospheric. Less visited than the major Wenshan attractions; a good destination for a half-day hike that feels far from the city.
address: Yinhe Cave, Wenshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Xianji Rock
description: A hilltop temple and rock formation in Wenshan District with views across the Jingmei and Xindian river valleys — a moderately demanding hiking trail leads to the summit rock from which the district takes its name. The temple at the top is a functional pilgrimage destination with resident monks; the surrounding hillside forest is typical of the sub-tropical vegetation that covers the southern Taipei hills.
address: Xianji Rock, Wenshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Grand Hotel Taipei
description: One of the most distinctive buildings in Taiwan — a 14-storey Chinese imperial palace-style hotel completed in 1973 on a forested hill above the Keelung River in Zhongshan District. The red pillars, golden roof tiles, and massive scale make it visible from much of the city. The secret spiral evacuation slide tunnels built into the hillside below the hotel are now a tourist attraction in their own right. The lobby and gardens are open to non-guests; the hilltop location provides good views.
address: No. 1, Zhongshan N Rd Sec 4, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei Expo Park
description: The former site of the 2010 Taipei International Flora Exposition, now a public park on the banks of the Jilong River in Zhongshan District. The event pavilions have been repurposed as exhibition and event spaces; the rose garden and riverside promenade are pleasant for a walk. The park connects the Grand Hotel area to the Yuanshan MRT station and is a good green corridor between the two.
address: No. 1, Yumen St, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Dajia Riverside Park
description: The main riverbank park along the Jilong River in Zhongshan District — a long green corridor with cycling paths, jogging tracks, and open lawns that connects a string of riverside parks running from the Guandu wetlands to the eastern edge of the city. The cycling infrastructure along the Jilong River is the best in Taipei; bikes can be rented from YouBike stations throughout. The park is most alive in the late afternoon when residents come to exercise.
address: Dajia Riverside Park, Zhongshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Guandu Nature Park
description: A managed wetland nature reserve at the confluence of the Danshui and Jilong rivers in northern Beitou — one of the most important bird watching sites in East Asia during the autumn and winter migration, when tens of thousands of migratory shorebirds and waterfowl use the tidal mudflats. The boardwalk trails through the mangroves and reed beds are accessible year-round; the visitor centre has good information on what to look for. Adjacent to the Guandu Temple.
address: No. 55, Guandu Rd, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Dahu Park
description: A large lake park in Neihu District built around the Dahu reservoir — weeping willows over the water, rowing boats for hire, jogging paths, and a covered pavilion used for tai chi and community events. The park is a genuine neighbourhood amenity rather than a tourist attraction; the crowd on weekends is Neihu families and joggers rather than visitors. The surrounding Neihu technology park makes it an accessible afternoon escape from the corporate district.
address: Dahu Park, Neihu District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Bihu Park
description: A small wetland and lake park in Neihu District — more naturalistic and quieter than the adjacent Dahu Park, with boardwalk trails through the reed beds and a shallow lake that supports water birds year-round. The park is connected to the Neihu cycling network. A good destination for a morning walk that has the feeling of countryside despite being inside the city limits.
address: Bihu Park, Neihu District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei Mosque
description: The Grand Mosque of Taipei, built in 1960 — the most important mosque in Taiwan, serving the city's Muslim community and providing halal certification for restaurants across the island. The building's white arches and green dome sit incongruously beautiful against the surrounding Da'an District streets. Visitors are welcome outside prayer times; remove shoes before entering. The neighbourhood around the mosque has a cluster of halal restaurants serving Indonesian, Malaysian, and Middle Eastern food.
address: No. 62, Xinsheng S Rd Sec 2, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: National Taiwan University
description: The most prestigious university in Taiwan — the main campus entrance on Roosevelt Road is lined with a canopy of royal palms that is one of the most pleasant walkways in Taipei. The campus is open to the public; the old Administration Building, the Drunken Moon Lake, and the agricultural land in the southern portion of the campus give it a scale and variety unusual for a city university. Gongguan Night Market and the Shida area are within walking distance.
address: No. 1, Sec. 4, Roosevelt Rd, Da'an District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Presidential Office Building
description: The seat of the Taiwanese government, built in 1919 as the Japanese Governor-General's Office — a Baroque Renaissance brick building with a 60-metre central tower that was once the tallest structure in Taipei. Free tours run on weekday mornings with advance booking; they include the main ceremonial rooms and a small historical exhibition about Taiwan's democratic transition. The building is most interesting as a political object: an imperial structure that became the home of a democracy.
address: No. 122, Chongqing S Rd Sec 1, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei Guest House
description: The official state guesthouse of Taiwan, built in 1901 in the French Second Empire style with a mansard roof, colonnaded portico, and formal gardens — one of the most ornate buildings in Taipei, designed by Moriyama Matsunosuke who also built the Presidential Office. Open to the public on designated weekends and national holidays; the interior, with its original parquet floors and period furnishings, is worth seeing. Check the official calendar for open dates.
address: No. 1, Kaifeng St, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Taipei North Gate
description: The only surviving city gate of Qing Dynasty Taipei in its original unaltered form — a squat defensive structure of grey brick that endured because the Japanese routed their road infrastructure around it rather than through it, unlike the other four gates which were rebuilt in a Chinese palace style. The gate sits as a small island in the middle of a busy road junction; a pedestrian underpass leads to it. The surrounding North Gate district has become a fashionable café neighbourhood.
address: Zhonghua Rd Sec 1 & Zhongxiao W Rd, Zhongzheng District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Zhinan Temple
description: A large Taoist temple complex on a forested hillside in Wenshan District dedicated to Lü Dongbin, one of the Eight Immortals — famous across Taiwan for the local superstition that couples who visit together will break up due to the deity's legendary jealousy. The temple compounds are spread across multiple levels of the hillside connected by steep staircases; the upper shrines have views across the Jingmei valley. Worth visiting for the setting and the architecture regardless of your relationship status.
address: No. 115, Zhinan Rd Sec 3, Wenshan District, Taipei, Taiwan

name: Guandu Temple
description: One of the most important Mazu temples in northern Taiwan — built into the basalt cliff face above the Danshui River estuary, with a cave shrine carved into the rock behind the main hall where worshippers file through by candlelight. The waterfront setting with fishing boats below and mangroves stretching toward the sea gives Guandu a geographic drama that most urban temples lack. The adjacent Guandu Nature Park is the best birdwatching site in Taipei. Guandu MRT is directly below.
address: No. 360, Zhixing Rd, Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan
